ABA.description: Evidence-based approach using principles of learning and behavior to increase helpful behaviors and decrease harmful ones. Gold-standard for ASD intervention.

techniques.name: Discrete Trial Training (DTT)
techniques.description: Structured one-on-one teaching using clear instructions, prompting, and reinforcement. Breaks skills into small, teachable steps.
techniques.indicators: Difficulty with structured learning, delayed milestones, limited response to verbal instructions

techniques.name: Natural Environment Teaching (NET)
techniques.description: Teaching skills in natural settings using child-initiated interests as learning opportunities.
techniques.indicators: Limited social engagement, reduced play skills, difficulty generalizing learned skills

techniques.name: Pivotal Response Training (PRT)
techniques.description: Targets pivotal areas (motivation, self-management, responding to multiple cues) to produce widespread behavioral improvements.
techniques.indicators: Low motivation, difficulty with self-regulation, limited response to social cues

techniques.name: Functional Behavior Assessment (FBA)
techniques.description: Systematic process to identify the purpose of challenging behaviors and develop replacement strategies.
techniques.indicators: Repetitive behaviors, self-stimulatory actions, behavioral rigidity, tantrums

techniques.name: Positive Behavior Support (PBS)
techniques.description: Proactive approach to prevent challenging behaviors by modifying the environment and teaching alternative behaviors.
techniques.indicators: Environmental triggers for challenging behavior, difficulty with transitions

techniques.name: Task Analysis & Chaining
techniques.description: Breaking complex skills into smaller sequential steps, teaching them through forward or backward chaining.
techniques.indicators: Difficulty with multi-step tasks, daily living skills delays

ESDM.description: Comprehensive early intervention combining ABA with developmental and relationship-based approaches. Delivered in natural play-based settings for toddlers and preschoolers.

techniques.name: Joint Attention Activities
techniques.description: Using shared play and social routines to build eye contact, shared enjoyment, and coordinated attention with caregivers.
techniques.indicators: Limited eye contact, reduced shared attention, difficulty following gaze

techniques.name: Imitation Training
techniques.description: Structured play to encourage copying actions, sounds, and facial expressions, building the foundation for social learning.
techniques.indicators: Limited imitation skills, reduced facial expression mirroring, delayed gesture use

techniques.name: Sensory Social Routines
techniques.description: Predictable social games (e.g., peek-a-boo, tickle games) that build anticipation, turn-taking, and social engagement.
techniques.indicators: Reduced social reciprocity, limited interaction initiation

techniques.name: Play-Based Communication
techniques.description: Using child-preferred toys and activities to create natural communication opportunities and expand language.
techniques.indicators: Speech delay, limited vocabulary, reduced communicative intent

techniques.name: Parent-Delivered ESDM
techniques.description: Training parents to implement ESDM strategies during daily routines (meals, bath, play) to maximize learning hours.
techniques.indicators: General developmental concerns, need for consistent home intervention

techniques.name: Motor Skill Integration
techniques.description: Incorporating fine and gross motor activities into social and communication goals.
techniques.indicators: Motor delays, difficulty with coordinated movement, atypical posture patterns

PECS.description: Augmentative/alternative communication system teaching individuals to communicate using picture symbols. Progresses through 6 phases from basic requesting to commenting.

techniques.name: Phase I: Physical Exchange
techniques.description: Child learns to exchange a single picture for a desired item with physical prompting and reinforcement.
techniques.indicators: No functional speech, limited communicative initiation, reliance on gestures only

techniques.name: Phase II: Distance & Persistence
techniques.description: Child learns to travel to communication board and exchange pictures across increasing distances.
techniques.indicators: Communication only when partner is adjacent, difficulty sustaining communication attempts

techniques.name: Phase III: Picture Discrimination
techniques.description: Child learns to select the correct picture from a field of options to request specific items.
techniques.indicators: Difficulty discriminating between items, inconsistent requesting

techniques.name: Phase IV: Sentence Structure
techniques.description: Child constructs sentences on a sentence strip (e.g., 'I want' + item picture).
techniques.indicators: Single-word communication, difficulty combining symbols, limited sentence use

techniques.name: Phase V: Responsive Requesting
techniques.description: Child answers 'What do you want?' using the picture system.
techniques.indicators: Under-responsive to questions, difficulty with conversational exchange

techniques.name: Phase VI: Commenting
techniques.description: Child spontaneously comments on environment using pictures (e.g., 'I see' + object).
techniques.indicators: Limited spontaneous communication, difficulty with expressive labeling

SensoryIntegration.description: Occupational therapy approach addressing sensory processing difficulties through structured sensory experiences to improve adaptive responses.

techniques.name: Sensory Diet Program
techniques.description: Personalized schedule of sensory activities throughout the day to maintain optimal arousal and attention.
techniques.indicators: Over/under-responsivity to sensory input, self-stimulatory behaviors, difficulty with attention

techniques.name: Deep Pressure Activities
techniques.description: Use of weighted blankets, compression garments, and firm touch to provide calming proprioceptive input.
techniques.indicators: Sensory seeking, difficulty calming down, tactile defensiveness

techniques.name: Vestibular Stimulation
techniques.description: Swinging, spinning, and balance activities to improve body awareness and motor planning.
techniques.indicators: Poor balance, gravitational insecurity, motion sensitivity

techniques.name: Visual-Motor Integration
techniques.description: Activities combining visual tracking with fine motor tasks to improve hand-eye coordination and visual processing.
techniques.indicators: Atypical gaze patterns, reduced eye tracking, difficulty with visual-motor tasks

SocialSkills.description: Structured programs teaching social interaction skills through modeling, role-play, video modeling, and peer-mediated strategies.

techniques.name: Social Stories
techniques.description: Short personalized stories describing social situations and expected behaviors, created to help the child understand social expectations.
techniques.indicators: Difficulty understanding social rules, unexpected responses in social situations

techniques.name: Video Modeling
techniques.description: Using video recordings of appropriate social behavior for the child to observe and imitate.
techniques.indicators: Limited social imitation, difficulty generalizing social skills

techniques.name: Peer-Mediated Intervention
techniques.description: Training typically-developing peers to facilitate social interactions and model appropriate social behavior.
techniques.indicators: Social isolation, difficulty initiating or maintaining peer interactions

techniques.name: Emotion Recognition Training
techniques.description: Teaching identification and understanding of emotions through facial expressions, body language, and tone of voice.
techniques.indicators: Difficulty reading facial expressions, limited emotional vocabulary, atypical facial affect

techniques.name: Turn-Taking & Cooperative Play
techniques.description: Structured practice in sharing, waiting, and cooperating during play activities with guided support.
techniques.indicators: Parallel play without interaction, difficulty sharing, limited cooperative behavior

A.title: Persistent deficits in social communication and social interaction across multiple contexts
subcriteria: A1: Deficits in social-emotional reciprocity (e.g., abnormal social approach, failure of back-and-forth conversation, reduced sharing of interests/emotions)
A2: Deficits in nonverbal communicative behaviors (e.g., poorly integrated verbal/nonverbal communication, abnormalities in eye contact and body language, deficits in understanding/use of gestures)
A3: Deficits in developing, maintaining, and understanding relationships (e.g., difficulty adjusting behavior to social contexts, difficulty sharing imaginative play, absence of interest in peers)

B.title: Restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior, interests, or activities
subcriteria: B1: Stereotyped or repetitive motor movements, use of objects, or speech
B2: Insistence on sameness, inflexible adherence to routines, ritualized patterns
B3: Highly restricted, fixated interests that are abnormal in intensity or focus
B4: Hyper- or hypo-reactivity to sensory input or unusual interest in sensory aspects of the environment

severity_levels.Level 1: Requiring support — noticeable impairments without support in place
severity_levels.Level 2: Requiring substantial support — marked deficits in verbal/nonverbal social communication
severity_levels.Level 3: Requiring very substantial support — severe deficits in verbal/nonverbal social communication

mchat_guidelines.description: Modified Checklist for Autism in Toddlers, Revised with Follow-Up (M-CHAT-R/F)

scoring.low_risk: Total score 0-2: no follow-up required unless surveillance indicates risk
scoring.medium_risk: Total score 3-7: administer follow-up. If score remains >= 2 after follow-up, refer for diagnostic evaluation
scoring.high_risk: Total score 8-20: immediate referral for diagnostic evaluation and early intervention

critical_items: Does your child look you in the eye?
Does your child respond when you call their name?
Does your child point to ask for something?
Does your child follow where you are looking?
Does your child pretend (e.g., feed a doll)?

face.clinical_relevance: Atypical facial expressions may indicate social-emotional reciprocity deficits (DSM-5 A1) and nonverbal communication deficits (DSM-5 A2)

behavior.clinical_relevance: Repetitive/atypical behavioral patterns relate to restricted/repetitive behaviors (DSM-5 B1, B2, B4)

questionnaire.clinical_relevance: Structured screening capturing parent-reported developmental concerns across all ASD domains

eye_tracking.clinical_relevance: Atypical gaze patterns suggest deficits in eye contact and shared attention (DSM-5 A2, A3)

pose.clinical_relevance: Atypical posture and movement patterns may indicate motor stereotypies (DSM-5 B1) or sensory processing differences (DSM-5 B4)

audio.clinical_relevance: Atypical speech prosody patterns (reduced pitch variability, unusual rhythm, increased pauses) relate to communication differences (DSM-5 A1, A2). Note: atypical prosody is a neurodevelopmental difference, not a deficit

eeg.clinical_relevance: Elevated theta/beta ratio and atypical interhemispheric coherence are neural biomarkers associated with autism. These reflect different neural processing patterns (neurodiversity), not pathology

neurodiversity_note: Autism is a form of neurodiversity with unique cognitive strengths including attention to detail, pattern recognition, systematic thinking, and intense focus. Intervention goals should be strength-based, supporting the individual's development while respecting their neurodevelopmental identity.
